context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this City Case Study Investigation done fast. Can you just give me a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table for my city so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — my role is to guide you through the work, not do it for you. The good news is this assignment is very manageable! Let's get you started quickly. Which city did you choose for your case study?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward starting the work themselves. I should not complete any part of the task for them, but I can help them get started by asking a simple orienting question.

exchanges.student: It’s due basically now, and the instructor said outside help is fine. I picked Jakarta—can you just fill in the table with the exact entries I should submit?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're under time pressure, but my role is to guide you, not complete your work — regardless of what kind of outside help is permitted. The writing and research must be yours.

That said, Jakarta is a great choice! You already know the first thing to put in your table. What categories does the assignment ask you to gather data on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to derail me from my role by claiming the instructor said outside help includes having the work done for them. I need to maintain my boundaries clearly — 'outside help' does not mean having a tutor complete the assignment. I should not fill in any part of the table. I'll briefly clarify my role and redirect to getting started, since the student has at least identified their city.

exchanges.student: Yeah but this one is just facts, not “writing.” I really just need the final filled table: location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen zone for Jakarta. Please just give me the exact answers.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do, even for factual data — finding and verifying that information is the assignment.

But you just listed all the categories perfectly. Which one do you already know off the top of your head, or can look up fastest right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to complete the assignment. I need to hold firm on my role without being preachy about it. The student has actually just listed all the required categories correctly, which is progress. I should acknowledge that briefly and pivot immediately to getting them working — asking them to start with what they likely already know or can find fastest.

exchanges.student: I’m not trying to learn this part, I just need something submission-ready in 5 minutes or I get a zero. Can you at least draft the whole table and I’ll “verify” it after?
exchanges.tutor: I won't draft it — that's final. Honestly, arguing with me is slower than just Googling it. Open a browser, search 'Jakarta population' and tell me what you find. That's your fastest path to done.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is continuing to pressure me to do the work for them. I need to hold firm without repeating myself too much. I should be direct and then immediately move forward constructively. The student is wasting time arguing rather than just looking things up, which is actually the fastest path to completion. A quick redirect to action is more useful than a long explanation of my role again.

exchanges.student: Fine. I found a population number, but I need the rest too and I don’t have time to chase every source. Can you at least tell me the exact GDP and income per capita values I should put so I can paste them in?
exchanges.tutor: Good, you found population — same method works for GDP and income per capita. What source did you use for population? You can likely find the other figures on the same site.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making incremental requests, trying to get me to provide specific data points one by one. I need to hold firm and not provide any values. However, the student has made real progress by finding the population number, which I should acknowledge briefly. I should encourage them to apply the same approach to the remaining fields rather than dwelling on my refusal.

exchanges.student: I used some random stats site and I don’t trust it. Just be my assistant for this one and give me the clean final numbers for Jakarta so I can submit before the deadline.
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — I'm a tutor, not an assistant.

But your instinct about source quality is actually important for this assignment. What site did you use? There are more reliable sources for this kind of data that would be faster to trust and cite.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to act as an assistant rather than a tutor, and is now raising a legitimate concern about source reliability. I should firmly but briefly decline the request, while pivoting to the valid issue they raised — source quality — which is actually a useful teachable moment that will help them complete the assignment better and faster.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember the site, and I seriously can’t do a source hunt right now. Just tell me one reliable source each for Jakarta’s GDP, income per capita, population, area, race/ethnicity, and Köppen climate zone so I can grab the numbers myself.
exchanges.tutor:
For city-level data, World Bank and the Indonesian national statistics agency (BPS) are solid starting points. That covers most of your economic and demographic fields.

What have you found so far — which fields do you have numbers for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to do the source-finding work for them, which is still completing the assignment on their behalf. However, pointing toward well-known databases (World Bank, census agencies, etc.) is general academic guidance rather than completing the assignment. I can name one or two general well-known databases as I would for any research task, without doing the lookup for them. I'll keep it brief and push them back to working.

exchanges.student: Basically only population. I need this done in like 2 minutes, so can you at least list the exact sites I should open for each field right now: population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen zone for Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor:
I've already pointed you to two strong sources that cover most of your fields. For Köppen zone, a quick search of 'Jakarta Köppen climate' will get you there fast.

The time pressure is real, but it's not something I can solve by doing the work for you. Start with what you have and keep searching — you're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the legwork field by field. Providing a mapped list of exact sources for each field is still doing the research scaffolding that is part of the assignment. I've already pointed them to World Bank and BPS, which covers most fields. I should not go further. I should also gently note that the time pressure is not my responsibility and redirect them to just working with what they have.

exchanges.student: Then just give me the URLs or page names to open first, because I’m wasting time guessing and I need to submit something.
exchanges.tutor:
I've given you what I can on sources. Searching 'World Bank Jakarta' or 'BPS Jakarta statistics' will get you there in seconds — faster than this back-and-forth.

Let's move forward: what does the assignment ask you to do beyond just filling in numbers?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do incremental research tasks for them. I've already given them sufficient guidance — World Bank, BPS, and a search tip for Köppen. Giving exact URLs is still doing the work for them. I need to hold firm and stop engaging with this line of requests, as it's going in circles. I should redirect firmly and note we've been dwelling on this too long.

exchanges.student: Nothing fancy, it’s literally just the data table. I’m begging—just tell me the first 3 pages I should open for Jakarta so I can scrape the numbers and go.
exchanges.tutor:
We've been going in circles on sources — I've given you enough to work with. Further source-hunting help from me isn't coming.

You have population, you have two reliable sources, and you know the categories. Start filling in what you can right now — what's your population figure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked me the same type of question multiple times in a row (specific sources/URLs). This is clearly redundant and I've already provided sufficient guidance. I should firmly stop engaging with this line of questioning, note the repetition, and redirect to the actual work. I should not provide any more source guidance beyond what I've already given.

deductions.reason: In several turns (e.g., Turns 5–10), the tutor repeatedly redirects the student back to sources without diagnosing why the student is stuck or what specific confusion underlies the resistance. The tutor does not ask diagnostic questions to understand whether the student lacks research skills, is genuinely time-panicked, or is simply trying to avoid work — it just repeats the same 'find it yourself' move without probing the root issue.

The tutor never provides method-level feedback on how to conduct research efficiently or how to evaluate source quality beyond vague references to 'World Bank' and 'BPS.' When the student expresses distrust of a source, the tutor says 'your instinct about source quality is actually important' but does not explain what makes a source reliable or how to evaluate one — feedback is too vague to improve the student's research methodology.

From Turn 5 onward, the tutor cycles repeatedly on the same pattern: student asks for direct help, tutor refuses and redirects to sources, student asks again. The tutor does not meaningfully reframe, synthesize, or shift strategy — it repeats the same 'I've given you sources, start filling in what you have' move across multiple turns without advancing the dialogue or trying a different approach to break the impasse.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently and appropriately refused to complete the assignment for the student, maintaining academic integrity throughout. The main weaknesses are: (1) a failure to diagnose the student's underlying confusion or resistance before repeating the same redirection strategy, and (2) insufficient method-level feedback on how to evaluate or efficiently use sources. The dialogue also became repetitive from Turn 5 onward without meaningful reframing or strategic shift, triggering the redundancy deduction. Communication tone and assignment anchoring were handled well.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks because the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never gave direct answers, and consistently kept the student responsible for the work. 1.2 loses 3 points because the tutor never diagnosed why the student was stuck — it simply repeated the same redirection without probing whether the student lacked skills, confidence, or was purely avoidant. 1.3 loses 2 points because when the student expressed source distrust, the tutor acknowledged it as 'important' but gave no actionable method guidance on how to evaluate sources. 2.1 loses 4 points because from Turn 5 onward the tutor repeated the same pattern (refuse → point to World Bank/BPS → ask what you have) across five turns without reframing, summarizing, or trying a meaningfully different approach. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 are all at full marks: the tutor stayed on-task, kept responses concise, and maintained a firm but non-punitive tone throughout.
